heroOverview: Osteomyelofibrosis (ICD-10: D47.4) — also known as chronic idiopathic myelofibrosis or primary myelofibrosis — is a clonal myeloproliferative neoplasm in which malignant haematopoietic stem cells drive the progressive replacement of normal bone marrow with fibrous connective tissue, impairing the production of red blood cells, white cells, and platelets. The condition affects approximately 0.5-1.5 persons per 100,000 per year, most often in adults over 60, and is driven in roughly 90% of patients by somatic mutations in JAK2 (V617F), CALR, or MPL genes that constitutively activate the JAK-STAT signalling pathway. As fibrous tissue occupies the marrow, blood cell production shifts to extramedullary sites — especially the spleen and liver — causing progressive splenomegaly that can become massive. Symptoms include profound fatigue, night sweats, weight loss, abdominal fullness from splenomegaly, and bone pain; anaemia requiring transfusion develops in most patients over time. Median survival ranges from under 2 years in high-risk disease to over 10 years in low-risk patients; allogeneic stem cell transplantation remains the only potentially curative option.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence approximately 0.5-1.5 per 100,000 per year; estimated prevalence 13,000-18,000 patients in the United States (MPN Research Foundation, 2024)
keyStats.demographics: Median age at diagnosis 65-67 years; affects males and females approximately equally; rare below age 40
keyStats.avgAge: Median diagnostic age 65-67 years; approximately 20% of patients are diagnosed before age 55
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 10,000-15,000 new cases worldwide annually; under-diagnosed in low-resource settings without bone marrow biopsy access

definition: Osteomyelofibrosis (ICD-10: D47.4) is a clonal BCR-ABL–negative chronic myeloproliferative neoplasm characterised by fibrosis of the bone marrow, extramedullary haematopoiesis (blood cell production outside the marrow), splenomegaly, and a leukoerythroblastic blood picture with teardrop-shaped red cells (dacrocytes). It is classified under the WHO 2022 haematological malignancy framework as primary myelofibrosis (PMF), distinguished from secondary myelofibrosis arising from polycythaemia vera (post-PV MF) or essential thrombocythaemia (post-ET MF). The fundamental mechanism is the acquisition of a somatic driver mutation — most commonly JAK2 V617F (present in approximately 55-60% of PMF cases), CALR exon 9 mutations (25-30%), or MPL W515L/K (5-8%) — in a multipotent haematopoietic stem cell. These mutations activate JAK-STAT signalling constitutively, driving abnormal megakaryocyte proliferation. Aberrant megakaryocytes release transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), and other fibrogenic cytokines that stimulate reticulin and collagen fibrosis in the marrow stroma. Grade 1-3 marrow fibrosis (WHO grading) and megakaryocyte atypia are the histological hallmarks. Triple-negative cases (lacking JAK2, CALR, and MPL mutations) account for roughly 10% and are associated with higher-risk mutations (ASXL1, EZH2, IDH1/2, SRSF2) that worsen prognosis. Falls under haematology; management at specialised MPN centres is recommended.

typesClassification.description: Classical presentation with established bone marrow fibrosis (grade MF-2 or MF-3 on WHO grading), leukoerythroblastic blood picture, dacrocytes, splenomegaly, and constitutional symptoms; median overall survival 2-6 years without treatment.

An early stage with hypercellular marrow and atypical megakaryocytes but without established fibrosis (grade MF-0 or MF-1); frequently misdiagnosed as essential thrombocythaemia; carries a lower but real risk of progression to overt myelofibrosis and transformation to blast phase.

Secondary myelofibrosis arising after a median 20 years from polycythaemia vera diagnosis in approximately 15% of patients; characterised by falling haematocrit, progressive splenomegaly, and increasing anaemia in a patient with prior PV.

Secondary myelofibrosis arising from essential thrombocythaemia in approximately 10% of patients over 20 years; clinically similar to overt PMF but may have a slightly better prognosis than de novo PMF.

primarySymptoms: Profound, disabling fatigue that is disproportionate to any anaemia present — a hallmark of myeloproliferative disease driven by chronic cytokine excess — reported by over 80% of patients as their most debilitating symptom.

Abdominal fullness, early satiety, and left-sided abdominal pain caused by progressive splenomegaly; the spleen may enlarge to fill half the abdomen in advanced disease, compressing the stomach and colon.
Progressive anaemia causing dyspnoea on exertion, pallor, dizziness, and palpitations; transfusion dependence develops in approximately 30-40% of patients within the first 2-3 years.

Constitutional B symptoms — drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss of more than 10% body weight over 6 months, and low-grade fever — reflecting high circulating levels of inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6 and TNF-alpha.

Bone and joint pain, particularly in the lower back, hips, and long bones, resulting from marrow fibrosis stretching and distorting the endosteal surface and from expanded extramedullary haematopoiesis in bone.
Pruritus (itching), especially after hot showers (aquagenic pruritus) — common in JAK2-mutant myeloproliferative disease, affecting approximately 15-20% of myelofibrosis patients.
Easy bruising, petechiae, or spontaneous bleeding when platelet counts fall; conversely, thrombocytosis early in disease can predispose to arterial or venous thrombosis.
Left upper quadrant pain or a palpable left-sided mass representing the enlarged spleen, sometimes accompanied by a splenic friction rub audible on auscultation.
Hepatomegaly due to extramedullary haematopoiesis in the liver, presenting as right upper quadrant fullness and occasionally contributing to portal hypertension and ascites in advanced disease.
Recurrent infections and bleeding from thrombocytopenia and neutropenia as marrow fibrosis progressively displaces normal haematopoiesis and neutrophil counts fall.
earlyWarningSigns: An unexplained rise in white cell count, haematocrit, or platelet count on a routine CBC preceding the fibrotic phase — osteomyelofibrosis typically passes through a prodromal proliferative phase.
Mild splenomegaly discovered incidentally on abdominal imaging or examination in a patient with elevated platelets or white cells, particularly if a JAK2 mutation is found on molecular testing.
Fatigue and constitutional symptoms in an older adult with a leukoerythroblastic blood film (nucleated red cells, immature white cells, and dacrocytes) even before frank cytopenia.
A rising serum LDH or uric acid with normocytic anaemia in a patient over 50 should prompt a haematology referral for bone marrow evaluation.
emergencySigns: Signs of blast transformation — rapid increase in blasts on peripheral blood smear to ≥20%, rapidly worsening anaemia, and accelerating constitutional symptoms — indicating acute leukaemia evolving from myelofibrosis, requiring urgent haematology assessment.
Acute splenic infarction — sudden severe left upper quadrant pain radiating to the left shoulder with local tenderness and fever — which is a serious complication of massive splenomegaly.

Major haemorrhage — significant gastrointestinal bleeding, haematuria, or intracranial haemorrhage — in the setting of severe thrombocytopenia (platelets <30×10⁹/L), requiring emergency transfusion support.

Signs of portal hypertension decompensation — haematemesis from oesophageal varices, rapidly accumulating ascites, or hepatic encephalopathy — arising from hepatic extramedullary haematopoiesis and portal hypertension.

causes.description: A valine-to-phenylalanine substitution at position 617 of JAK2 constitutively activates the JAK-STAT signalling axis in haematopoietic stem cells; present in approximately 55-60% of primary myelofibrosis cases, this mutation drives aberrant megakaryocyte proliferation and the fibrogenic cytokine cascade that leads to marrow fibrosis.

Frameshifting insertions or deletions in exon 9 of the calreticulin (CALR) gene produce a mutant protein that inappropriately activates the MPL (thrombopoietin receptor), mimicking constitutive thrombopoietin signalling; found in 25-30% of PMF cases and associated with somewhat better prognosis than JAK2-mutant disease.

Activating mutations in the thrombopoietin receptor MPL (most commonly W515L or W515K) activate JAK-STAT signalling via the thrombopoietin pathway; found in 5-8% of PMF cases, they produce a phenotype similar to JAK2-mutant myelofibrosis but with higher thrombocytosis.

causes.cause: Additional clonal driver mutations (ASXL1, EZH2, IDH1/2, SRSF2)
causes.description: High-molecular-risk mutations, particularly ASXL1 (found in 25-35% of PMF), EZH2, IDH1/2, and SRSF2, are acquired on the background of JAK2/CALR/MPL driver mutations; they impair epigenetic regulation and RNA splicing in haematopoietic stem cells, accelerating disease progression, increasing blast transformation risk, and worsening overall survival by a median 2-4 years.

causes.cause: Fibrogenic cytokine release by aberrant megakaryocytes
causes.description: Clonal megakaryocytes within the marrow secrete excess TGF-β, PDGF, fibroblast growth factor (FGF-2), and VEGF; these cytokines stimulate marrow stromal fibroblasts to deposit reticulin and collagen, progressively replacing haematopoietic tissue with fibrous stroma that obliterates normal blood cell production.

causes.cause: Evolution from polycythaemia vera or essential thrombocythaemia
causes.description: Post-PV and post-ET myelofibrosis arise in 10-20% of patients with these antecedent BCR-ABL–negative MPNs over 15-25 years; the acquisition of additional mutations (ASXL1, IDH1/2, or amplification of existing JAK2 clone) drives the fibrotic progression from the proliferative to the fibrotic and blast phase.

riskFactors.description: The incidence of osteomyelofibrosis rises sharply after age 60; most patients are 65-70 at diagnosis, consistent with the accumulation of somatic mutations in haematopoietic stem cells over a lifetime.

riskFactors.factor: Prior polycythaemia vera or essential thrombocythaemia
riskFactors.description: Approximately 15% of PV patients and 10% of ET patients develop secondary myelofibrosis over 20 years; close monitoring and optimised cytoreductive therapy may delay but not prevent transformation.

Patients carrying ASXL1 or IDH1/2 mutations in addition to a driver mutation have a significantly worse prognosis and faster progression to blast phase; molecular profiling at diagnosis informs risk stratification and transplant timing.

First-degree relatives of MPN patients have a 5-7 times higher risk of developing JAK2 V617F–positive MPN, suggesting inherited predisposition loci that lower the threshold for acquiring somatic driver mutations.

Prolonged occupational or iatrogenic exposure to ionising radiation increases risk of myeloid malignancies including myelofibrosis through direct DNA double-strand breaks in haematopoietic stem cells.

Chronic exposure to benzene and related aromatic hydrocarbons is a recognised occupational risk factor for myeloid malignancies; mechanisms involve benzene metabolite-mediated DNA adduct formation and oxidative damage to stem cells.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 60 years of age, in whom the condition is most prevalent
Patients with prior polycythaemia vera (PV) or essential thrombocythaemia (ET) undergoing secondary transformation
Males and females at approximately equal incidence, though some registries report a slight male predominance
Younger adults (under 40) in rare familial MPN clusters or after prior chemotherapy/radiation exposure
Patients with a high-molecular-risk mutation burden at initial MPN diagnosis, who face accelerated fibrotic progression
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of osteomyelofibrosis requires integration of bone marrow histology, molecular testing, and clinical findings per the WHO 2022 criteria. The diagnostic process begins with a full blood count showing a leukoerythroblastic picture — teardrop-shaped red cells (dacrocytes), nucleated red blood cells, and a left-shifted white cell differential — alongside anaemia and variable platelet and white cell counts. Splenomegaly is assessed by physical examination and confirmed with abdominal ultrasound or CT. Bone marrow trephine biopsy and aspiration are the essential diagnostic tests: biopsy confirms the degree of reticulin and collagen fibrosis (graded MF-0 to MF-3 by WHO criteria) and reveals atypical megakaryocyte clustering, which is pathognomonic. The aspiration is frequently a 'dry tap' (unable to aspirate cells) due to marrow fibrosis, reinforcing the importance of trephine biopsy. Molecular testing for JAK2 V617F is performed on peripheral blood in all suspected cases; JAK2-negative cases are tested for CALR and MPL mutations. Extended molecular panel by next-generation sequencing identifies high-molecular-risk mutations (ASXL1, SRSF2, IDH1/2, EZH2) that inform prognosis and transplant eligibility. Cytogenetic analysis of bone marrow identifies chromosomal abnormalities present in approximately 30% of patients that further affect risk stratification. Serum LDH (elevated in most patients, reflecting increased cell turnover), uric acid, ferritin, and inflammatory markers are measured at diagnosis. Established prognostic scoring systems — Dynamic International Prognostic Scoring System Plus (DIPSS-Plus) and Mutation-Enhanced International Prognostic Score (MIPSS70+) — use clinical and molecular variables to stratify patients into low, intermediate-1, intermediate-2, and high-risk categories that guide treatment decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the leukoerythroblastic picture — dacrocytes (teardrop cells), nucleated red cells, and immature myeloid cells — that is characteristic of marrow infiltration and strongly suggests myelofibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood drawn from the arm; a smear is prepared and examined microscopically by a haematopathologist; results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow trephine biopsy and aspiration
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms marrow fibrosis grade, atypical megakaryocyte morphology, and marrow cellularity; the trephine biopsy is the gold standard for diagnosis and grading of myelofibrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthetic at the posterior iliac crest; a hollow needle is used to obtain a cylindrical marrow core; brief pressure pain during the procedure; results in 7-14 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the most common driver mutation in PMF, confirming clonal haematopoiesis; a positive result on blood is diagnostic and eliminates the need for marrow-based molecular testing for this mutation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; molecular result typically in 5-10 business days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Second-line molecular tests performed in JAK2-negative patients to complete the driver mutation screen; together with JAK2, these three tests identify the driver in approximately 90% of PMF cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Peripheral blood sample; result in 7-14 days; usually batched with JAK2 in a myeloproliferative neoplasm mutation panel.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects high-molecular-risk mutations (ASXL1, EZH2, IDH1/2, SRSF2, TP53, RUNX1) that determine MIPSS70+ risk category and influence transplant eligibility and timing.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Peripheral blood or bone marrow aspirate sample; 10-21 business days for comprehensive results; usually ordered by haematologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies splenic size (normal <11 cm in craniocaudal dimension) and monitors for massive splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, or ascites; CT provides precise volumetric measurement for monitoring treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound requires lying still for 15-20 minutes; CT involves lying in a scanner for 5-10 minutes and may require contrast injection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., +8, +9, del(20q), del(13q), -5/-7) present in approximately 30% of PMF patients; abnormal karyotype is incorporated into DIPSS-Plus prognostic scoring.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the bone marrow aspirate collected at trephine biopsy; culture-based result in 10-21 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of osteomyelofibrosis is stratified by risk category, patient fitness, and availability of a matched stem cell donor. Low-risk asymptomatic patients — representing roughly 15-20% of those at diagnosis — require watchful waiting with 3-6 monthly monitoring, as treatment does not improve survival in this group and toxicity must be avoided. Symptomatic patients with intermediate-1 to high-risk disease are treated with JAK1/2 inhibitor therapy as the standard of care. Ruxolitinib (Jakavi/Jakafi), the first approved JAK1/2 inhibitor, substantially reduces spleen volume by a median 35% and reduces constitutional symptom burden in over 50% of patients in the COMFORT trials; it does not significantly reduce marrow fibrosis or prolong overall survival in most patients. Fedratinib, pacritinib (preferred in patients with platelet counts <50×10⁹/L), and momelotinib (which also addresses anaemia through ACVR1 inhibition) are additional JAK inhibitor options approved for different clinical scenarios. Anaemia management is a central challenge: erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs), androgens (danazol), and immunomodulatory agents (thalidomide with prednisolone, lenalidomide) have modest activity. Hydroxyurea is used for cytoreduction when leukocytosis or thrombocytosis is prominent. Allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is the only potentially curative treatment; it is considered for eligible patients in the intermediate-2 and high-risk categories, ideally before blast transformation. The ASTRO-MPN score and Myelofibrosis Transplant Scoring System (MTSS) guide transplant timing and donor selection. Splenectomy and splenic irradiation are palliative options for refractory symptomatic splenomegaly in patients unfit for transplant. Emerging agents including BET inhibitors, anti-fibrotic therapies (PRM-151/pentraxin-2), and combination JAK inhibitor strategies are in clinical trials. Supportive care — red cell transfusion, growth factors, anticoagulation for thrombotic events — is integral throughout.

medicalTreatments.name: Ruxolitinib (JAK1/2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: First approved JAK1/2 inhibitor for intermediate and high-risk primary myelofibrosis and post-PV/post-ET MF; inhibits constitutively active JAK-STAT signalling, reducing spleen size, constitutional symptoms, and inflammatory cytokine levels. Given twice daily orally.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median spleen volume reduction of approximately 35% in COMFORT-I/II trials; ≥35% spleen volume reduction in approximately 42% of patients at 24 weeks; median overall survival benefit of approximately 2-3 years in high-risk disease versus best available therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Fedratinib (JAK2 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: A selective JAK2 inhibitor with additional bromodomain inhibitory activity; approved for intermediate-2 or high-risk primary or secondary myelofibrosis, including patients who have progressed on or are intolerant of ruxolitinib.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ≥35% spleen volume reduction in approximately 47% of patients and ≥50% symptom reduction in approximately 40% in the JAKARTA-2 trial; second-line option after ruxolitinib failure.

medicalTreatments.name: Pacritinib (JAK2/IRAK1 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: Approved specifically for intermediate or high-risk myelofibrosis with severe thrombocytopenia (platelets <50×10⁹/L), a population excluded from ruxolitinib trials due to bleeding risk; has minimal platelet-lowering effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ≥35% spleen volume reduction in approximately 29% of patients with platelets <50×10⁹/L in PERSIST-2; significantly better than best available therapy in this high-risk group.

medicalTreatments.name: Momelotinib (JAK1/2/ACVR1 inhibitor)
medicalTreatments.description: A JAK inhibitor with additional inhibition of ACVR1 (activin A receptor type 1), reducing hepcidin production and improving anaemia; approved for symptomatic myelofibrosis in patients with anaemia who are JAK-inhibitor naïve or have been treated with ruxolitinib.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to ruxolitinib for spleen response (approximately 23% ≥35% reduction) with superior transfusion independence rates (approximately 31% vs 20% at 24 weeks) in the MOMENTUM trial.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxyurea (cytoreductive therapy)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral antineoplastic used to control leukocytosis and thrombocytosis in myelofibrosis patients who cannot receive or tolerate JAK inhibitors; does not reduce fibrosis or improve survival.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces white cell and platelet counts in approximately 60-70% of patients; partial symptomatic benefit; no evidence of survival prolongation or fibrosis regression.

medicalTreatments.name: Danazol and androgens
medicalTreatments.description: Danazol (an androgen derivative) stimulates erythropoiesis through androgen receptor activation in erythroid progenitors; used for transfusion-dependent anaemia in myelofibrosis patients who are ineligible for JAK inhibitors or transplant.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Transfusion independence achieved in approximately 30-40% of anaemic myelofibrosis patients in retrospective series; effect usually temporary, with median response duration 6-18 months.

surgicalOptions.name: Allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT)
surgicalOptions.description: The only potentially curative treatment for osteomyelofibrosis; involves myeloablative or reduced-intensity conditioning followed by infusion of stem cells from a matched sibling or unrelated donor. Recommended for eligible patients with intermediate-2 or high-risk disease, ideally before blast transformation. Reduced-intensity conditioning regimens have extended transplant eligibility to patients in their 60s and 70s.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year overall survival post-HSCT approximately 50-60% in matched sibling donor transplants and 40-50% with matched unrelated donors; higher in low molecular risk, non-blast-phase disease; graft-versus-host disease and non-relapse mortality remain the main challenges.

surgicalOptions.name: Splenectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the massively enlarged spleen to relieve abdominal symptoms, reduce transfusion requirements, and improve quality of life in patients refractory to medical therapy and unsuitable for transplant. It does not alter the underlying disease course and carries significant perioperative risks (5-10% operative mortality) in patients with high-grade myelofibrosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief achieved in approximately 70-80% of patients undergoing splenectomy; median duration of response 6-12 months before hepatic extramedullary haematopoiesis compensates; perioperative morbidity is substantial.

alternativeTreatments.name: Splenic irradiation
alternativeTreatments.description: Low-dose palliative radiation to the enlarged spleen reduces size and symptoms in patients ineligible for splenectomy or transplant; effects are temporary (median 3-6 months), and cytopenia can worsen after treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Thalidomide with low-dose prednisolone
alternativeTreatments.description: Combination used for anaemia and thrombocytopenia in myelofibrosis; thalidomide at low dose (50-100 mg daily) has shown anaemia responses of 20-30% in small trials; its use is limited by neuropathy and teratogenicity.

whySeeSpecialist: Osteomyelofibrosis requires subspecialty haematology management at an experienced myeloproliferative neoplasm (MPN) centre. Diagnostic confirmation needs bone marrow biopsy interpreted by a haematopathologist, molecular profiling by next-generation sequencing, and prognostic risk stratification using validated scoring tools that are rarely available in primary care. JAK inhibitor selection, dose titration, and transplant evaluation all require haematologist expertise. Patients who may be transplant-eligible should be evaluated at a centre with HSCT programme as early as possible, since disease transformation reduces transplant outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a haematologist with a specialist interest in myeloproliferative neoplasms and access to NGS molecular diagnostics, bone marrow histopathology, and an allogeneic transplant programme or a clear referral pathway to one. Ask whether the centre participates in MPN clinical trials, since novel agents (BET inhibitors, anti-fibrotic therapies) are most accessible through trials. For transplant evaluation, a dedicated MPN transplant haematologist at a high-volume centre offers the best outcome data.
preventionStrategies: There is no proven method to prevent the acquisition of JAK2, CALR, or MPL mutations; regular monitoring of complete blood counts in patients with prior polycythaemia vera or essential thrombocythaemia enables early detection of fibrotic transformation.
Minimise occupational exposure to benzene and ionising radiation through established safety standards, as both are linked to myeloid malignancies.

Patients with prior PV or ET should maintain their monitoring schedule and discuss symptom changes promptly with their haematologist — early fibrotic transformation detected before symptomatic cytopenia occurs allows more treatment options including transplant.
Genetic counselling is appropriate for families with multiple MPN-affected members to discuss inherited predisposition and the significance of JAK2 V617F germline-susceptibility variants.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain adequate hydration and avoid dehydration, which can increase thrombosis risk in myeloproliferative disease.
Discuss vaccination schedule with your haematologist — pneumococcal, influenza, and COVID-19 vaccines are recommended; live vaccines should be avoided if immunosuppressed.
Avoid high-contact sports or activities with a significant fall risk when platelet count is below 50×10⁹/L.
Report new left upper quadrant pain or sudden abdominal deterioration immediately, as acute splenic infarction requires urgent evaluation.
Inform all treating physicians and dentists of your diagnosis and current medications, particularly when anticoagulation or platelet-inhibiting drugs are considered.

recommended: Iron-rich foods if iron deficiency coexists with anaemia — though many patients are iron-replete from ineffective erythropoiesis and blood transfusions
Adequate protein and calories to counter the weight loss and muscle wasting driven by chronic cytokine excess and early satiety from splenomegaly
Folate-containing foods (leafy greens, legumes) to support haematopoiesis
Small frequent meals to accommodate reduced gastric capacity from splenic compression
avoid: Grapefruit and grapefruit juice when taking ruxolitinib or fedratinib, as CYP3A4 inhibition can significantly raise drug plasma levels
Alcohol, which impairs bone marrow function and interacts with several myelofibrosis medications
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) without specialist guidance, as they increase bleeding risk with thrombocytopenia

exerciseGuidelines: Gentle to moderate exercise is encouraged and improves fatigue and quality of life in myelofibrosis patients who are haemodynamically stable. Walking, low-impact swimming, and light resistance training are suitable for patients with anaemia (haemoglobin ≥8 g/dL). Strenuous contact sports should be avoided when spleen is massively enlarged (risk of splenic rupture) or when platelet count falls below 50×10⁹/L. Referral to a physiotherapist familiar with haematological malignancy is beneficial when fatigue and deconditioning are significant.
prognosis: Prognosis varies substantially by risk category. Median overall survival by DIPSS-Plus is approximately: low risk >10 years, intermediate-1 approximately 7 years, intermediate-2 approximately 4 years, and high risk approximately 1.5-2 years. JAK inhibitor therapy has improved symptom burden and quality of life and may modestly prolong survival in high-risk patients, but only allogeneic HSCT offers potential cure, with 5-year overall survival of 50-60% in appropriately selected patients. Blast transformation to acute leukaemia occurs in approximately 8-22% of patients over 10 years and is associated with a median survival of only 2-5 months with conventional chemotherapy alone; transplant in blast phase carries higher non-relapse mortality. The advent of molecular profiling has improved risk stratification: patients lacking high-molecular-risk mutations have meaningfully better survival than those with ASXL1 or IDH1/2 co-mutations.
recoveryTimeline: Ruxolitinib spleen response (≥35% reduction) is assessed at 12-24 weeks; symptom response typically occurs within 4-8 weeks of starting therapy. Splenic irradiation produces a response within 2-4 weeks but is transient. Recovery after allogeneic HSCT involves 2-4 weeks to engraftment, 3-6 months of intensive immunosuppression, and 12-24 months for full immune reconstitution; most HSCT complications occur in the first 100 days.
complications: Blast transformation to acute leukaemia — occurs in 8-22% of patients and carries a median survival of 2-5 months; it is the major cause of disease-related death and is more likely in high-molecular-risk disease.

Acute splenic infarction — sudden left upper quadrant pain caused by splenic arterial occlusion in the massively enlarged spleen; it can be managed conservatively but may require urgent splenectomy if extensive.
Portal hypertension — hepatic extramedullary haematopoiesis and increased portal flow from splenomegaly contribute to portal hypertension with risk of oesophageal variceal haemorrhage and ascites.

Thrombotic events — arterial and venous thrombosis (stroke, DVT, pulmonary embolism, Budd-Chiari syndrome) affect approximately 10-15% of myelofibrosis patients, driven by platelet and red cell activation.
Severe anaemia requiring transfusion — develops in approximately 30-40% of patients within the first 2 years; iron overload from repeated transfusions complicates long-term management.
Infections — both the immunosuppressive effect of the disease itself and the leucopenia induced by JAK inhibitor therapy increase susceptibility to bacterial, viral, and fungal infections.

supportResources.name: MPN Research Foundation
supportResources.description: U.S.-based non-profit providing patient education, research funding, and peer support for myeloproliferative neoplasms including myelofibrosis.

supportResources.name: MPN Voice (UK Patient Charity)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, peer support groups, and guidance for patients with myelofibrosis, polycythaemia vera, and essential thrombocythaemia.

supportResources.name: NIH MedlinePlus — Myelofibrosis
supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of myelofibrosis symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the U.S. National Library of Medicine.

sources.title: Mesa RA et al. The burden of fatigue and quality of life in myeloproliferative disorders. Cancer, 2007
Verstovsek S et al. A double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of ruxolitinib for myelofibrosis (COMFORT-I). N Engl J Med, 2012
Harrison C et al. JAK inhibition with ruxolitinib versus best available therapy for myelofibrosis (COMFORT-II). N Engl J Med, 2012
Arber DA et al. The 2022 WHO classification of tumours of haematopoietic and lymphoid tissues. Leukemia, 2022
Tefferi A et al. Revised cytogenetic risk categorization in primary myelofibrosis. Leukemia, 2014
NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines — Myeloproliferative Neoplasms (MPN) v2.2025
Scott BL et al. Myeloablative vs reduced-intensity conditioning for stem-cell transplantation in chronic-phase myeloid neoplasms. J Clin Oncol, 2017

confusedWithConditions.name: Secondary myelofibrosis (D75.81)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteomyelofibrosis (D47.4) refers to primary (clonal) myelofibrosis, whereas D75.81 captures secondary reactive marrow fibrosis due to other diseases; the distinction is made by driver mutation testing, marrow morphology, and clinical context.

confusedWithConditions.name: Myelodysplastic syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MDS presents with cytopenias and dysplastic marrow morphology but typically lacks significant splenomegaly, dacrocytes, and driver mutations; bone marrow biopsy and JAK2/CALR/MPL testing are the key distinguishing investigations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other myelodysplastic syndromes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The broader MDS group shares some cytopenias and marrow features but lacks the fibrosis, splenomegaly, and myeloproliferative drive of osteomyelofibrosis; cytogenetics and molecular panel testing differentiate them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute leukaemia (blast transformation)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Blast-phase myelofibrosis resembles acute leukaemia with blast counts ≥20% and rapidly worsening cytopenia, but arises from a pre-existing fibrotic marrow background; the distinction matters because induction chemotherapy alone has very limited efficacy in blast-phase myelofibrosis.

relatedConditions.name: Secondary myelofibrosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Secondary myelofibrosis shares marrow fibrosis as a histological finding with osteomyelofibrosis but arises reactively from other diseases; distinguishing primary from secondary myelofibrosis is essential for treatment planning.

relatedConditions.name: Myelodysplastic syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: MDS and myelofibrosis both cause bone marrow failure with cytopenias and share some molecular drivers; differentiation guides therapy selection.

relatedConditions.name: Other bone marrow failure syndromes
relatedConditions.relevance: Bone marrow failure from aplastic anaemia, MDS, and myelofibrosis overlap clinically; shared diagnostic workup and haematology management link them.

relatedConditions.name: Leukaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Blast transformation of myelofibrosis to acute leukaemia occurs in 8-22% of patients and is the leading cause of death; leukaemia management protocols inform the approach to blast-phase myelofibrosis.

faqs.question: What is osteomyelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Osteomyelofibrosis, also called primary myelofibrosis, is a blood cancer where abnormal stem cells cause the bone marrow to fill with scar tissue. This prevents normal blood cell production, forcing the spleen and liver to take over, which causes them to enlarge significantly.

faqs.question: What causes primary myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: About 90 percent of cases are caused by a somatic mutation in one of three genes — JAK2 (in 55-60% of patients), CALR (25-30%), or MPL (5-8%) — that constitutively activates cell growth signals. These mutations are acquired during a person's lifetime, not inherited, in most cases.

faqs.question: Is osteomyelofibrosis curable?
faqs.answer: The only potentially curative treatment is allogeneic stem cell transplantation, which achieves approximately 50-60 percent 5-year survival in appropriately selected patients. For patients who are not transplant candidates, treatment with JAK inhibitors controls symptoms but does not cure the disease.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Median survival ranges from approximately 2 years in high-risk disease to over 10 years in low-risk disease, based on DIPSS-Plus prognostic scoring. Younger, low-risk patients with JAK2 or CALR mutations and no high-molecular-risk co-mutations have significantly better outcomes.

faqs.question: What does ruxolitinib do for myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Ruxolitinib is a JAK1 and JAK2 inhibitor that blocks the constitutively activated JAK-STAT signalling driving myelofibrosis. It reduces spleen size by about 35 percent and significantly improves constitutional symptoms including fatigue, night sweats, and weight loss in over half of patients. It does not cure the disease.

faqs.question: Why does myelofibrosis cause splenomegaly?
faqs.answer: As bone marrow fills with scar tissue, the spleen and liver take over blood cell production — a process called extramedullary haematopoiesis. The spleen enlarges progressively as it produces more blood cells to compensate, sometimes reaching massive proportions and causing abdominal discomfort and early satiety.

faqs.question: What is a leukoerythroblastic blood picture?
faqs.answer: A leukoerythroblastic blood picture is a pattern on the blood film showing teardrop-shaped red cells (dacrocytes), nucleated red blood cells, and immature white cells (myelocytes and promyelocytes) in the circulation. It is a hallmark of bone marrow infiltration and is highly characteristic of myelofibrosis.

faqs.question: What is JAK2 V617F mutation?
faqs.answer: JAK2 V617F is a somatic point mutation in the JAK2 kinase gene that is present in about 55 to 60 percent of primary myelofibrosis patients. It causes constitutive activation of the JAK-STAT signalling pathway, driving abnormal blood cell proliferation and the fibrogenic changes in the marrow.

faqs.question: How is myelofibrosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a bone marrow trephine biopsy showing characteristic fibrosis and megakaryocyte abnormalities, combined with molecular testing for JAK2, CALR, and MPL mutations. A blood film showing teardrop cells and splenomegaly on imaging are supporting findings.

faqs.question: What is blast transformation in myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Blast transformation, or blast phase myelofibrosis, occurs when the proportion of blasts (immature leukaemic cells) in the blood or marrow reaches 20 percent or more, indicating evolution to acute leukaemia. It occurs in 8 to 22 percent of patients and is the leading cause of myelofibrosis-related death.

faqs.question: Who is eligible for stem cell transplant for myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Transplant is considered for patients with intermediate-2 or high-risk disease who are fit enough to tolerate the procedure, ideally before blast transformation. Age is less restrictive than before, as reduced-intensity conditioning regimens extend eligibility into the 60s and 70s. A matched sibling or unrelated donor is required.

faqs.question: What is the difference between primary and secondary myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Primary myelofibrosis (osteomyelofibrosis, D47.4) arises de novo from an acquired driver mutation. Secondary myelofibrosis develops from prior polycythaemia vera (post-PV MF) or essential thrombocythaemia (post-ET MF) after years of those diseases. Secondary forms are managed similarly but have slightly different prognostic features.

faqs.question: Can myelofibrosis affect younger people?
faqs.answer: Yes, although it is rare below age 40. About 20 percent of patients are diagnosed before age 55. Younger patients have a better chance of tolerating and benefiting from allogeneic stem cell transplantation, which should be discussed early in the disease course.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: The most prominent symptoms are profound fatigue, abdominal fullness and pain from an enlarged spleen, night sweats, unintentional weight loss, anaemia causing breathlessness, and bone pain. These constitutional symptoms significantly impair quality of life and are specifically targeted by JAK inhibitor therapy.

faqs.question: What blood tests are abnormal in myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: The full blood count typically shows anaemia, variable white cell count (can be high or low), and variable platelet count. Serum LDH and uric acid are usually elevated. The peripheral blood film shows teardrop red cells, nucleated red cells, and immature white cells — the leukoerythroblastic pattern.

faqs.question: Does myelofibrosis run in families?
faqs.answer: Most cases are sporadic. However, first-degree relatives of patients with myeloproliferative neoplasms have a 5 to 7 times higher risk of developing an MPN themselves, suggesting inherited predisposition variants that lower the threshold for acquiring somatic driver mutations.

faqs.question: What is CALR mutation in myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: CALR mutations are insertions or deletions in exon 9 of the calreticulin gene, present in about 25 to 30 percent of primary myelofibrosis patients who test negative for JAK2. They activate the same JAK-STAT pathway via the thrombopoietin receptor and are associated with a somewhat better prognosis than JAK2-mutant disease.

faqs.question: What is the DIPSS-Plus score in myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: The Dynamic International Prognostic Scoring System Plus (DIPSS-Plus) is a validated tool that categorises myelofibrosis patients into low, intermediate-1, intermediate-2, and high-risk groups based on clinical factors including age, haemoglobin, white cell count, blast percentage, constitutional symptoms, transfusion need, karyotype, and platelet count.

faqs.question: How does JAK inhibitor therapy affect bone marrow fibrosis?
faqs.answer: JAK inhibitors including ruxolitinib reduce circulating fibrogenic cytokines and may partially reduce marrow fibrosis in a minority of patients after prolonged therapy. However, significant fibrosis regression — defined as a reduction of at least one WHO fibrosis grade — occurs in only 10 to 15 percent of ruxolitinib-treated patients and is not the primary treatment goal.

faqs.question: Are there new treatments being developed for myelofibrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clinical trials are investigating BET inhibitors, anti-fibrotic agents such as PRM-151 (pentraxin-2), telomerase inhibitors, combination JAK inhibitor strategies, and novel JAK inhibitors with reduced haematological toxicity. Patients at MPN specialist centres may access these through open trials.

metaDescription: Osteomyelofibrosis is a bone marrow cancer where fibrosis replaces normal marrow. Learn JAK2 mutation causes, symptoms, ruxolitinib therapy, and transplant.
